title: 4-on-the-Floor Kick
description: Build the most fundamental drum pattern in UK garage: a kick on every beat, clap on 2 and 4, off-beat hats. Isolated from all harmony so you can focus purely on the groove.
what_youll_learn: How to set up a Drum Rack from scratch
What 4-on-the-floor means and why it works
How to layer kick, clap, and hi-hat into a locked groove
Basic kick EQ: punch without mud

steps.title: Set up your project
steps.instruction: Open a new Ableton project. Set BPM to 132. Set time signature to 4/4. Save it as 'rep-01-four-floor'.
steps.why: 132 BPM sits in the IC Slow Burner range — enough energy to feel urgent without rushing. 4/4 means 4 beats per bar; this is the grid every other element will lock to.
steps.tip: Cmd+S (Mac) or Ctrl+S (Win) to save immediately. Ableton doesn't auto-save.

steps.title: Load a Drum Rack and find your kick
steps.instruction: Create a new MIDI track. Drag 'Drum Rack' from Instruments > Drum Rack onto it. Search Splice or your sample library for a short, punchy kick — something under 500ms with a clear low-end thump. Drag it onto the C1 pad.
steps.why: C1 (MIDI note 36) is the standard kick position in a Drum Rack. A punchy kick — not boomy or long — sits cleanly in a garage mix. IC Slow Burner uses a tight kick that doesn't swell into the bass range.
steps.tip: Preview samples in Ableton's browser with the headphone icon before committing. You're listening for: short decay, clear attack, defined pitch center.

steps.title: Program the 4-on-the-floor kick
steps.instruction: Create a 2-bar MIDI clip on the Drum Rack track. Open the piano roll. Place a C1 note on every beat: positions 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4. Set each note length to 1/8 note. Set velocity to 100 for all.
steps.why: 4-on-the-floor = kick on all 4 beats of every bar. This creates relentless forward momentum. At 132 BPM the kicks arrive every 454ms — just fast enough to feel driving. This is the foundation IC Slow Burner is built on.
steps.tip: Hold Shift while drawing notes in the piano roll to snap to the grid. Set the grid to 1/4 to make beat placement precise.

steps.title: Add clap on beats 2 and 4
steps.instruction: Find a clap or snare sample. Drag it onto the D1 pad. In the piano roll, place D1 notes on positions 1.2 and 1.4 in each bar. Set velocity to 95.
steps.why: The backbeat (2 and 4) is where the groove is anchored. Clapping on 2 and 4 is instinctive — every music listener does it. The clap gives the listener something to lock onto while the kick drives forward. IC uses a tight clap; MPH uses a looser UK clap.
steps.tip: The clap should be slightly quieter than the kick — it's the rhythmic anchor, not the loudest element. Start at velocity 95 and adjust by ear.

steps.title: Program off-beat closed hi-hats
steps.instruction: Find a closed hi-hat sample. Drag it onto the F#1 pad. In the piano roll, place F#1 notes on every off-beat: 1.1.3, 1.2.3, 1.3.3, 1.4.3 (the '+' of each beat — exactly halfway between beats). That's 8 per bar. Set velocity to 75.
steps.why: Off-beat hats land between kicks, filling the rhythmic space. Because they never land on a beat, they create motion without competing with the kick. IC Slow Burner runs hats on every off-beat — this is what makes the groove feel continuous rather than just 4 thumps per bar.
steps.tip: Set the grid to 1/8 in the piano roll to snap precisely to off-beats. Hats should feel like they're pushing the kick forward, not competing with it.

steps.title: Add an open hat accent on beat 2.5
steps.instruction: Find an open hi-hat sample. Drag it onto the G#1 pad. Place a single G#1 note per bar on position 1.2.3 (the '+' of beat 2). Set it to a short note length (1/8). Set velocity to 85. In the Drum Rack pad view, set the open hat's Choke Group to 1 — and the closed hat's Choke Group to 1 as well.
steps.why: The open hat accent on beat 2.5 is a classic UK garage texture — it adds 'air' between the backbeats. The choke group means a closed hat immediately cuts the open hat ring, just like a real hi-hat. Without choke groups, both sustain simultaneously and sound unrealistic.
steps.tip: Choke groups are in the pad view bottom panel — the small number box next to each pad. Both open and closed hat need the same number (e.g. '1') to choke each other.

steps.title: EQ the kick
steps.instruction: Click the kick pad in the Drum Rack to select its chain. Add EQ Eight from Audio Effects to this chain. Activate band 1 as a High Pass at 40Hz — this removes sub rumble. Add a bell band at 65Hz, Q=1.5, boost +3dB for punch. If the kick sounds boxy or muddy, add a bell cut at 250Hz around -4dB.
steps.why: Sub rumble below 40Hz takes up headroom without adding audible weight. The 65Hz boost adds the physical kick thump you feel in a club. 250Hz is the 'cardboard box' frequency — cutting it tightens the kick. IC's kick is present but never bloated.
steps.tip: Toggle the EQ on/off (the power button) to compare with and without. If you can't hear a difference, the cut/boost is too subtle — increase it until it's obvious, then back off slightly.

steps.title: Loop and listen
steps.instruction: Set your loop brace over bars 1–2. Hit play and loop. Close your eyes and listen for: does the kick lock with the off-beat hats? Does the clap feel grounded on 2 and 4? Adjust velocities until the groove feels right, not mechanical.
steps.why: Drum programming is ear work. The velocities you set by hand never feel as natural as what you hear when you tune them. Small adjustments — kick at 105 instead of 100, hat at 70 instead of 75 — change the feel significantly. This is the rep: loop, listen, adjust.
steps.tip: If the groove feels stiff, try applying a light swing to the MIDI clip. Right-click the clip > select a Swing groove from the Groove Pool. Start at 54% swing.
